cuisine_type: 中餐 Omakase (counter-seating contemporary Chinese, board-front style)
address: Shanghai (Huangpu) — exact street address not surfaced; original space was the former 佐々 (Sasa) omakase fine-dining venue
signature_dishes: chef-curated 中餐 Omakase tasting (specific course-by-course names not surfaced in sources)
vibe_and_space: Counter-seating (板前) format applied to Chinese cuisine — patrons watch the chef plate course-by-course. Inherited the physical space from the former 佐々 Japanese omakase fine-dining place, deliberately preserving the high-end Japanese-style format but swapping the cuisine to Chinese. Phone-recording restrictions and a 10+ minimum-age policy have been reported.
one_paragraph_recommendation: The canonical worked example of '中餐 Omakase as a category' in Shanghai — opened Jan 2022 in the former 佐々 Japanese omakase space, deliberately keeping the counter-seating board-front format but applying it to Chinese cuisine. Promoted from Black Pearl 一钻 to 二钻 in 2026 (one of 5 Shanghai 升钻 entries — a signal of institutional momentum, not just maintenance). Also a Michelin one-star. Per-person ~¥2,000-3,000, reservations weeks-ahead, no recording / no-young-children policies. Recommend ONLY for a deliberate special-occasion 'I want to try 中餐 Omakase as a format' visit — there's no casual case. Open question: is the Chinese-Omakase category itself sustainable, or is it a one-off format experiment that worked because the space and reservation system were inherited from a Japanese predecessor? Track whether other Shanghai venues attempt the same format in 2026-27.
contradictions: Strong institutional authority (2钻 升钻 + Michelin 1-star) vs persistent 公众号 critique that the format is over-priced for the actual cooking ('中华大冤种' label in some Chinese-internet reviews). The format-vs-cooking gap is the open question: are diners paying for the Omakase theatre or for the food? Black Pearl + Michelin agree the cooking is at one-star quality; the cost framing suggests the theatre is also priced in.
